by
  have h1 : Set A ∼ Set B := by
    -- Use the fact that A and B are equinumerous to show that their power sets are equinumerous.
    have h2 : A ∼ B := h
    have h3 : U ∼ V := by
      -- Since A and B are sets of U and V respectively, we can use the fact that A and B are equinumerous to show that U and V are equinumerous.
      -- However, this step is not directly necessary as we can instead use the set restriction.
      -- The proof can be simplified by directly using the set equinumerosity.
      have h4 : A ∼ B := h2
      exact h4.ofRestrictions
    -- Use the fact that U and V are equinumerous to show that their power sets are equinumerous.
    -- However, since we don't know if A and B are the entire sets U and V, we need to adjust this approach.
    -- The correct approach is to use the set equinumerosity to get the power set equinumerosity.
    have h5 : Set A ∼ Set B := by
      -- Use the fact that if two sets are equinumerous, their power sets are equinumerous.
      -- We use the fact that A and B are equinumerous to get the bijection between their power sets.
      apply h2.setOf
    exact h5
  -- Use the fact that Set A and Set B are equinumerous to conclude that 𝒫 A and 𝒫 B are equinumerous.
  have h6 : 𝒫 A ∼ 𝒫 B := by
    -- Since Set A ∼ Set B, we can use this to get the equinumerosity of their power sets.
    simpa [Set.setOf_mem_setOf] using h1
  exact h6

by
  have h₁ : (A : Type) ∼ (B : Type) := by
    exact h
  
  have h₂ : Set (A : Type) ∼ Set (B : Type) := by
    have h₃ : (A : Type) ∼ (B : Type) := h₁
    -- Use the fact that the powersets of equivalent types are equivalent
    have h₄ : Set (A : Type) ∼ Set (B : Type) := by
      -- Use the fact that the powersets of equivalent types are equivalent
      have h₅ : (A : Type) ∼ (B : Type) := h₃
      -- Use the fact that the powersets of equivalent types are equivalent
      exact Set_equinum_powerset
    -- The result follows from the above steps
    exact h₄
  
  have h₃ : Set (A : Type) ∼ 𝒫 A := by
    have h₄ : Set (A : Type) ≃ 𝒫 A := by
      -- Define the equivalence between Set A and 𝒫 A
      let f : Set (A : Type) → 𝒫 A := fun s => ⟨s, by exact Set.Subset.refl _⟩
      let g : 𝒫 A → Set (A : Type) := fun s => s.val
      refine' ⟨f, g, _, _, _⟩ <;> simp_all [Set.ext_iff]
      <;> aesop
    -- Use the equivalence to get the equinumerosity
    exact h₄.equinum
  
  have h₄ : Set (B : Type) ∼ 𝒫 B := by
    have h₅ : Set (B : Type) ≃ 𝒫 B := by
      -- Define the equivalence between Set B and 𝒫 B
      let f : Set (B : Type) → 𝒫 B := fun s => ⟨s, by exact Set.Subset.refl _⟩
      let g : 𝒫 B → Set (B : Type) := fun s => s.val
      refine' ⟨f, g, _, _, _⟩ <;> simp_all [Set.ext_iff]
      <;> aesop
    -- Use the equivalence to get the equinumerosity
    exact h₅.equinum
  
  have h₅ : 𝒫 A ∼ 𝒫 B := by
    calc
      𝒫 A ∼ Set (A : Type) := h₃.symm
      _ ∼ Set (B : Type) := h₂
      _ ∼ 𝒫 B := h₄
  
  exact h₅

  have h2 : Set A ∼ 𝒫 A := by
    have h₂ : Set A ∼ 𝒫 A := by
      apply Set.equinum_powerset
    exact h₂
  
  have h3 : Set B ∼ 𝒫 B := by
    have h₃ : Set B ∼ 𝒫 B := by
      apply Set.equinum_powerset
    exact h₃
  
  have h4 : 𝒫 A ∼ 𝒫 B := by
    have h₄ : 𝒫 A ∼ 𝒫 B := by
      -- Use the transitivity of equinumerosity to connect the powersets of A and B
      calc
        𝒫 A ∼ Set A := by
          -- The powerset of A is equinumerous to the set of subsets of A
          exact h2.symm
        _ ∼ Set B := by
          -- The set of subsets of A is equinumerous to the set of subsets of B
          exact h1
        _ ∼ 𝒫 B := by
          -- The set of subsets of B is equinumerous to the powerset of B
          exact h3.symm
    exact h₄
  
  exact h4

  have h₁ : Set A ∼ Set B := by

    <;> assumption

    have h₃ : (𝒫 A : Set (Set U)) ∼ (𝒫 B : Set (Set V)) := by
      -- Use the fact that the powerset of A is equivalent to the powerset of B

      simpa [Set.ext_iff] using Set_equinum_powerset B |>.trans (Set_equinum_powerset A).symm
    -- Cast the equivalence to the correct type.
    exact h₃
  exact h₂
